title: Distance Formula
content: The distance between two points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) in a plane is given by sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2). This is derived from the Pythagorean theorem applied to the horizontal and vertical displacements between the two points.
example: Distance between (1,2) and (4,6) = sqrt((4-1)^2 + (6-2)^2) = sqrt(9+16) = sqrt(25) = 5.

title: Section Formula
content: The coordinates of the point dividing the line segment joining (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) in the ratio m:n internally are ((mx2 + nx1)/(m+n), (my2 + ny1)/(m+n)). For external division, replace + with - in the denominator and numerator signs accordingly: ((mx2 - nx1)/(m-n), (my2 - ny1)/(m-n)).
formula_latex: P = \left( \frac{m x_2 + n x_1}{m + n},\ \frac{m y_2 + n y_1}{m + n} \right)
example: Point dividing the segment from (2,3) to (8,9) in the ratio 2:1 internally = ((2*8 + 1*2)/3, (2*9 + 1*3)/3) = (18/3, 21/3) = (6, 7).

title: Midpoint Formula
content: The midpoint of a line segment joining two points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) is the special case of the section formula where the ratio is 1:1. It gives the point that is equidistant from both endpoints.
example: Midpoint of segment joining (3, 5) and (7, 11) = ((3+7)/2, (5+11)/2) = (5, 8).

title: Area of a Triangle using Coordinates
content: The area of a triangle formed by three vertices (x1, y1), (x2, y2), and (x3, y3) can be computed using the determinant method. The absolute value ensures a positive area regardless of the ordering of vertices. If the area equals zero, the three points are collinear.
formula_latex: \text{Area} = \frac{1}{2} \left| x_1(y_2 - y_3) + x_2(y_3 - y_1) + x_3(y_1 - y_2) \right|

title: Collinearity of Three Points
content: Three points (x1, y1), (x2, y2), and (x3, y3) are collinear if and only if the area of the triangle formed by them is zero. Equivalently, the determinant formed by their coordinates equals zero. This is a fundamental test used in many JEE problems.

title: Slope of a Line
content: The slope (m) of a line passing through two points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) measures the steepness and direction of the line. A positive slope indicates the line rises from left to right, a negative slope indicates it falls. A horizontal line has slope 0 and a vertical line has undefined slope. The slope also equals tan(theta) where theta is the angle the line makes with the positive x-axis.
example: Slope of line through (2,3) and (5,9) = (9-3)/(5-2) = 6/3 = 2. The inclination angle = arctan(2) ≈ 63.43°.

title: Equations of a Line (Various Forms)
content: A straight line can be expressed in several standard forms: (1) Slope-intercept: y = mx + c, (2) Point-slope: y - y1 = m(x - x1), (3) Two-point form: (y-y1)/(y2-y1) = (x-x1)/(x2-x1), (4) Intercept form: x/a + y/b = 1, (5) Normal form: x cos(alpha) + y sin(alpha) = p, (6) General form: ax + by + c = 0. Choosing the right form simplifies problem-solving significantly in JEE.
example: Line with x-intercept 3 and y-intercept 4: x/3 + y/4 = 1, which simplifies to 4x + 3y = 12.

title: Angle Between Two Lines
content: The acute angle between two lines with slopes m1 and m2 is found using the tangent formula. If the lines are perpendicular, m1*m2 = -1 (the denominator becomes zero and theta = 90°). If the lines are parallel, m1 = m2 and theta = 0°. This formula is extremely important for JEE problems involving bisectors and loci.
example: Angle between y = 2x + 1 (m1=2) and y = 3x - 1 (m2=3): tan(theta) = |2-3|/|1+6| = 1/7, so theta = arctan(1/7) ≈ 8.13°.

title: Distance from a Point to a Line
content: The perpendicular distance from a point (x1, y1) to the line ax + by + c = 0 is given by the formula below. This is used extensively in finding the distance between parallel lines (take any point on one line and find its distance to the other), radius of inscribed circles, and optimization problems.
formula_latex: d = \frac{|a x_1 + b y_1 + c|}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2}}

title: Family of Lines
content: The equation L1 + lambda*L2 = 0 represents the family of all lines passing through the point of intersection of lines L1 = 0 and L2 = 0, where lambda is a parameter. This concept is powerful for JEE problems because it avoids explicitly computing the intersection point. Any line through the intersection can be written in this form for some real value of lambda.
formula_latex: (a_1 x + b_1 y + c_1) + \lambda (a_2 x + b_2 y + c_2) = 0
example: Family of lines through intersection of x + y - 1 = 0 and 2x - y + 3 = 0: (x+y-1) + lambda(2x-y+3) = 0. For the member passing through origin, put (0,0): (-1) + lambda(3) = 0, so lambda = 1/3.

title: General Equation of a Circle
content: The general equation of a circle is x^2 + y^2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0, where the center is (-g, -f) and the radius is sqrt(g^2 + f^2 - c). For a valid circle, the condition g^2 + f^2 - c > 0 must hold. The standard form (x-h)^2 + (y-k)^2 = r^2 has center (h,k) and radius r.
formula_latex: x^2 + y^2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0, \quad r = \sqrt{g^2 + f^2 - c}
example: Circle x^2 + y^2 - 6x + 4y - 12 = 0 has 2g=-6 so g=-3, 2f=4 so f=2. Center = (3,-2), radius = sqrt(9+4+12) = sqrt(25) = 5.

title: Tangent to a Circle
content: The equation of the tangent to the circle x^2 + y^2 = r^2 at a point (x1, y1) on the circle is xx1 + yy1 = r^2. For the general circle x^2 + y^2 + 2gx + 2fy + c = 0, the tangent at (x1,y1) is xx1 + yy1 + g(x+x1) + f(y+y1) + c = 0. A line y = mx + c is tangent to x^2 + y^2 = r^2 if c^2 = r^2(1 + m^2), known as the condition of tangency.
formula_latex: x x_1 + y y_1 = r^2 \quad \text{(tangent at } (x_1, y_1) \text{ on } x^2+y^2=r^2\text{)}
example: Tangent to x^2 + y^2 = 25 at (3,4): 3x + 4y = 25. Verify: slope = -3/4, and at (3,4): 9+16 = 25. Correct.

title: Normal to a Circle
content: The normal to a circle at any point on it always passes through the center of the circle. For a circle with center (h, k), the normal at a point (x1, y1) on the circle is the line joining (x1, y1) and (h, k). This is because the radius is always perpendicular to the tangent at the point of tangency.
example: Normal to x^2 + y^2 = 25 at (3,4): the line passes through (3,4) and center (0,0), so slope = 4/3. Equation: y = (4/3)x.

title: Chord of Contact
content: The chord of contact is the line joining the two points of tangency drawn from an external point to a circle. From an external point (x1, y1), the chord of contact of the circle x^2 + y^2 = r^2 has the equation xx1 + yy1 = r^2 (same form as the tangent equation, but the point is external). This concept extends to conics as well and is a frequent JEE topic.
formula_latex: T = 0 \implies x x_1 + y y_1 = r^2 \quad \text{(for } x^2 + y^2 = r^2\text{)}
example: Chord of contact from (5, 5) to x^2 + y^2 = 16: 5x + 5y = 16, i.e., x + y = 16/5.

title: Family of Circles and Radical Axis
content: The family of circles passing through the intersection of circles S1 = 0 and S2 = 0 is S1 + lambda*S2 = 0 (lambda != -1). The radical axis of two circles S1 = 0 and S2 = 0 is the locus of points having equal power with respect to both circles, given by S1 - S2 = 0. The radical axis is always perpendicular to the line joining the centers. For three circles, the three radical axes are concurrent at the radical center.
example: Radical axis of x^2+y^2-4x-6=0 and x^2+y^2+2x-8=0: subtracting gives -4x-6-2x+8=0 => -6x+2=0 => x=1/3.

title: Standard Equation of a Parabola
content: A parabola is the locus of a point equidistant from a fixed point (focus) and a fixed line (directrix). The standard form y^2 = 4ax has vertex at origin, focus at (a,0), directrix x = -a, axis along the x-axis, and latus rectum of length 4a. Other standard forms: y^2 = -4ax (opens left), x^2 = 4ay (opens up), x^2 = -4ay (opens down).
example: For y^2 = 12x: 4a = 12, so a = 3. Focus = (3,0), directrix: x = -3, latus rectum = 12.

title: Parametric Form and Tangent to a Parabola
content: For the parabola y^2 = 4ax, any point on it can be written parametrically as (at^2, 2at). The equation of the tangent at parameter t is ty = x + at^2. The tangent at a point (at1^2, 2at1) and at (at2^2, 2at2) intersect at the point (at1*t2, a(t1+t2)). The condition for a line y = mx + c to be tangent to y^2 = 4ax is c = a/m.
example: Tangent to y^2 = 8x at t=1: point = (2, 4). Tangent: 1*y = x + 2, i.e., y = x + 2. Verify: slope = 1, and y = mx + a/m = x + 2. Correct.

title: Focal Chord and Properties of a Parabola
content: A focal chord of y^2 = 4ax is any chord passing through the focus (a, 0). If one end is at parameter t1, the other end is at t2 where t1*t2 = -1. The length of the focal chord at parameter t is a(t + 1/t)^2. The semi-latus rectum is the harmonic mean of the segments of any focal chord. Tangents drawn at the endpoints of a focal chord are perpendicular and meet on the directrix.
example: For y^2 = 4x (a=1), focal chord at t=2: other end at t=-1/2. Points: (4,4) and (1/4, -1). Length = a(t+1/t)^2 = 1*(2+0.5)^2 = 6.25.

title: Standard Equation of an Ellipse
content: An ellipse is the locus of a point whose sum of distances from two fixed points (foci) is constant (2a). The standard equation is x^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 = 1 (a > b > 0), with center at origin, major axis along x-axis of length 2a, minor axis of length 2b. The relationship b^2 = a^2(1 - e^2) connects the semi-axes with eccentricity e. Foci are at (+-ae, 0) and directrices at x = +-a/e.

title: Tangent to an Ellipse
content: The tangent to the ellipse x^2/a^2 + y^2/b^2 = 1 at a point (x1, y1) is xx1/a^2 + yy1/b^2 = 1. In parametric form, the point (a cos(theta), b sin(theta)) has tangent (x cos(theta))/a + (y sin(theta))/b = 1. A line y = mx + c is tangent to the ellipse if c^2 = a^2*m^2 + b^2 (condition of tangency).
formula_latex: \frac{x x_1}{a^2} + \frac{y y_1}{b^2} = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad c^2 = a^2 m^2 + b^2

title: Properties of an Ellipse
content: Key properties: (1) Sum of focal distances for any point on the ellipse = 2a. (2) The product of perpendicular distances from the foci to any tangent equals b^2. (3) The latus rectum has length 2b^2/a. (4) The auxiliary circle has equation x^2 + y^2 = a^2. (5) Eccentric angle theta relates the ellipse to its auxiliary circle via (a cos(theta), b sin(theta)). (6) The director circle (locus of intersection of perpendicular tangents) is x^2 + y^2 = a^2 + b^2.
formula_latex: \text{Latus Rectum} = \frac{2b^2}{a}, \quad \text{Director Circle: } x^2 + y^2 = a^2 + b^2
example: For x^2/25 + y^2/9 = 1: a=5, b=3. Latus rectum = 2(9)/5 = 18/5. Director circle: x^2 + y^2 = 34.

title: Standard Equation of a Hyperbola
content: A hyperbola is the locus of a point whose absolute difference of distances from two foci is constant (2a). The standard equation is x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1, with transverse axis along x-axis of length 2a and conjugate axis of length 2b. Here b^2 = a^2(e^2 - 1), eccentricity e > 1. Foci are at (+-ae, 0). The conjugate hyperbola is y^2/b^2 - x^2/a^2 = 1.

title: Asymptotes of a Hyperbola
content: The asymptotes of the hyperbola x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1 are the lines y = (b/a)x and y = -(b/a)x. They pass through the center and are the diagonals of the rectangle formed by x = +-a and y = +-b. A rectangular hyperbola has a = b, giving perpendicular asymptotes y = +-x. The equation of asymptotes is x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 0. Importantly, the combined equation of the hyperbola, its asymptotes, and the conjugate hyperbola differ only in the constant term.
example: Asymptotes of x^2/9 - y^2/4 = 1: y = +-2x/3. These lines are approached by the hyperbola at infinity but never touched.

title: Tangent to a Hyperbola
content: The tangent to the hyperbola x^2/a^2 - y^2/b^2 = 1 at a point (x1, y1) is xx1/a^2 - yy1/b^2 = 1. In parametric form using (a sec(theta), b tan(theta)), the tangent is (x sec(theta))/a - (y tan(theta))/b = 1. A line y = mx + c is tangent if c^2 = a^2*m^2 - b^2. The director circle (locus of perpendicular tangent intersection) is x^2 + y^2 = a^2 - b^2, which exists only when a > b.
formula_latex: \frac{x x_1}{a^2} - \frac{y y_1}{b^2} = 1, \quad c^2 = a^2 m^2 - b^2
example: Tangent to x^2/16 - y^2/9 = 1 with slope 2: c^2 = 16(4) - 9 = 55, so c = +-sqrt(55). Lines: y = 2x +- sqrt(55).

title: Rectangular Hyperbola (xy = c^2)
content: The rectangular hyperbola xy = c^2 is a special form where the asymptotes are the coordinate axes. Any point on it can be written as (ct, c/t). The tangent at (ct, c/t) is x/t + yt = 2c. The normal at this point is xt^3 - yt - ct^4 + c = 0. The eccentricity of a rectangular hyperbola is always sqrt(2). This form appears frequently in JEE problems.
example: For xy = 4 (c=2), point at t=1: (2, 2). Tangent: x/1 + y(1) = 4, i.e., x + y = 4. Verify: at (2,2), 2+2=4. Correct.

title: Direction Cosines and Direction Ratios
content: Direction cosines (l, m, n) of a line are the cosines of the angles it makes with the positive x, y, and z axes respectively, satisfying l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1. Direction ratios (a, b, c) are any set of numbers proportional to the direction cosines: l = a/sqrt(a^2+b^2+c^2), etc. The direction ratios of a line joining points (x1,y1,z1) and (x2,y2,z2) are (x2-x1, y2-y1, z2-z1).
formula_latex: l^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1, \quad l = \frac{a}{\sqrt{a^2 + b^2 + c^2}}

title: Distance Between Two Points in 3D
content: The distance formula in three dimensions extends the 2D formula by including the z-coordinate term. It gives the length of the line segment joining two points in space. This is fundamental to all 3D geometry computations including finding the radius of a sphere, edge lengths, and verifying geometric shapes in space.
example: Distance from (1,2,3) to (4,6,3) = sqrt(9 + 16 + 0) = sqrt(25) = 5.

title: Equation of a Plane
content: A plane in 3D can be expressed in several forms: (1) General form: ax + by + cz + d = 0, where (a,b,c) is the normal vector. (2) Normal form: lx + my + nz = p, where (l,m,n) are DCs of the normal and p is the perpendicular distance from origin. (3) Intercept form: x/a + y/b + z/c = 1. (4) Point-normal form: a(x-x1) + b(y-y1) + c(z-z1) = 0. The distance from point (x1,y1,z1) to plane ax+by+cz+d=0 is |ax1+by1+cz1+d|/sqrt(a^2+b^2+c^2).
formula_latex: ax + by + cz + d = 0, \quad d = \frac{|ax_1 + by_1 + cz_1 + d|}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2+c^2}}
example: Plane through (1,2,3) with normal (2,3,6): 2(x-1)+3(y-2)+6(z-3)=0 => 2x+3y+6z=26. Distance from origin = 26/sqrt(4+9+36) = 26/7.

title: Equation of a Line in 3D
content: A line in 3D can be expressed in: (1) Symmetric/Cartesian form: (x-x1)/a = (y-y1)/b = (z-z1)/c, passing through (x1,y1,z1) with direction ratios (a,b,c). (2) Vector form: r = a + lambda*b, where a is a position vector of a point on the line and b is the direction vector. (3) Two-point form: (x-x1)/(x2-x1) = (y-y1)/(y2-y1) = (z-z1)/(z2-z1). The angle between two lines is determined by the angle between their direction vectors.
example: Line through (1,2,3) with DRs (2,3,-1): (x-1)/2 = (y-2)/3 = (z-3)/(-1). At parameter t=1: point (3, 5, 2).

title: Angle Between a Line and a Plane, and Skew Lines Distance
content: The angle theta between a line with DRs (a1,b1,c1) and a plane with normal (a2,b2,c2) satisfies sin(theta) = |a1*a2+b1*b2+c1*c2|/(sqrt(a1^2+b1^2+c1^2)*sqrt(a2^2+b2^2+c2^2)). The shortest distance between two skew lines r = a1 + lambda*b1 and r = a2 + mu*b2 is |(a2-a1).(b1 x b2)|/|b1 x b2|. Two lines are coplanar if this distance is zero. These concepts are critical for JEE Advanced.
example: Angle between line with DRs (1,1,1) and plane 2x+y-z=5 (normal (2,1,-1)): sin(theta) = |2+1-1|/(sqrt(3)*sqrt(6)) = 2/sqrt(18) = 2/(3*sqrt(2)), theta = arcsin(sqrt(2)/3).
